text_content: My Maine Coon lived to be 19.5 years old, broke my heart when he passed. They don't need to be huge to be a great pet. Aileron was 18 pounds in his prime. He could stand with back feet on the floor and get his nose above the counter top. He always had his place at the dinner table. He would sit and watch us eat and would never beg. One night we were having better cheddar hotdogs (stuffed with cheese) and when I bit into mine it squirted melted cheese into Aileron's face. He calmly cleaned his face at the table and never complained. Don't under estimate the female side of the breed. They are smaller but have every attribute of these fine feline companions.

text_content: We adopted a 32 pound Maine coon mix when his previous family moved to a complex that didn't allow pets. Nolan Ryan (aka Nollie) quickly turned into a Mama's boy who shared the bed while hubby worked night shift and stretched out on me in the recliner - with his back paws touching my feet and his front paws hugging my neck. We couldn't leave meals unattended because Nollie would stretch his front paws onto the dining table and scoot plates, pizza boxes, whatever, to the edge and onto the floor. He loved to go outside on a leash and roll in the catnip patch I planted, but would dash into my arms whenever a truck passed by (but cars didn't scare him, go figure. 🤷) Nollie was such a sweet, toddler sized, big baby of a cat and we still miss him dearly some 20 years after he crossed the 🌈🌉. RIP, "Papa Bear Nollie"

Romeo, the tiger I do believe will be given sanctuary once he grows bigger into his adult stage and be with his own kind. He is not a pet, but this young man considers him family as well as Romeo. Romeo thinks of him as a parental figure and family. The day they have to part will be devastating, but once he adjusts to his new environment it will be for the best. Of course this young man will visit when he can and the reunions will be amazing. Obviously, owning tigers are illegal unless you are known as a rescuer, who rehabilitates and gives sanctuary to certain types of animals for a period of time then transfers them to other qualified well known animal sanctuaries that have the money to do so. 

All in said, please support your local, well-known, and national sanctuaries and rescue places, because it helps their mission to feed, supply medical care, daily care, rescue more animals, advertise, and raise awareness world-wide even local. Thank you!

video_description: visit f1savannahkittens.com to learn about our kittens.
text_content:
I have a serval. She has a very large room and an enclosure. She is happy and she sleeps under my covers with me. The most important thing is to always respect her and in turn, she will love you. 
I also don’t play with her a certain way due to her claws. She is a great companion and has been my therapy cat for 5 years. 
I cannot work so I spend all day and night with her. 

Servals are wild animals and this must be kept in consideration. Definitely do not get a serval if your job could move you out of state and if you have kids. These cats get stressed easily and they can die. I waited 20 years until I settled down to buy one. And never plan to take a vacation ever again. No one is equipped with handling her.

text_content: I too had wanted a monkey since 6th grade. 30 years later I made a friend at work and told her of my lifelong dream. Her reaction was "No. No you don't." She had been a monkey owner and told of all the bad and hard things that could and did happen. All the struggles, all the messes, all the expense.  The tears shed and the phenomenal amount of patience required. She owned this monkey in the pre-internet days so I'm sure it was a learn as you go experience. I had to accept that I might not have this amount of dedication in me. I still love monkeys and I'm thankful for you YouTube so I can watch Xander and all the other sweet monkeys play and grow.

text_content: I'm a new subscriber that's been watching those horrible channels where people adopt "supposedly" orphaned baby monkeys. I'm absolutely appalled at how some of these people treat these little babies coming out of the wild. A few seem to really love them and care for them and actually keep them in their family once they grow into adults. But these "domesticated" monkeys seem to me to be nothing like their wild relatives and wouldn't be able to survive in the wild in a normal monkey troup. Being in a domestic setting truly changes their natures. Another thing I noticed is that babies raised by families that have outdoor spaces to let their pet monkeys roam around in seem much happier than those that are confined to an indoor space. So I'm going to watch your channel regularly now so I can understand better what SHOULD be done with these beautiful babies!!! Thank you for all you do to truly help them!!

Years ago, kinkajou were sold in pet stores in Washington DC, my mom and dad felt so horrible for these beautiful creatures that they took two of them home and raised them from babies. when I was born to up to two years old, the kinkajou became very protective over me.

Fortunately, a ban on owning them without being a rehabilitation center, my parents who were wildlife rehabilitators, but did not have a rehabilitation center, relocated to the National Smithsonian Zoo in Washington DC where they lived their lives out and as they got older, I got to visit them quite frequently. My parents name is still on a brick near the enclosure.

video_title: Living with an African Grey: What It’s Really Like After 10 Years
video_description: Living with an African Grey: What It's Really Like After 10 Years We are going to answer 10 questions that you should think about ...
text_content: I have had my AG since he was a baby, 25 yrs now and I love him to much.  Max is so funny, does all the bird things and he is my door bell.  No one can come to the door without him letting out a very loud high pitched whistle.  He does not take to anyone but me.  One thing I would advise to anyone is do let let your parrot be unsupervised as they will chew up everything.  And yes, if you rearrange the furniture they get a little disoriented.  He will eat anything that he sees me eat and actually prefers people food to seeds.  Lucky for him, I eat a lot of veggies.  I have two other parrots, a mealy Amazon and a red wing, all talk and keep each other company.  They are fun and wonderful companions but do require a lot of care.  There are times I wish I had ear plugs because when they start their screeching it can be over bearing, especially when company comes and we try to have a conversation, they want to be part of it too!  Parrots are not for everyone and people need to realize that they require lots of cleaning, and time, not to mention the cost of food.  They make lots of noise and can be destructive if unsupervised.  I have had birds since I was 18 yrs old, started with Cocktails and progressed to bigger Parrots.  I am old now and do worry about what I will do if something happens to me.

video_description: Welcome to my parrot educational channel, where you will find entertaining family vlogs, funny talking parrot videos, and get to ...
text_content: I have one CAG that I have wanted for years. I got him when he was 13w-14w old (he just turned 1) he is my first parrot. I had done tons of research over about a 2 year time span of learning everything I could. I decided to add one to my family.  There were a lot of people who frowned not only on me getting a baby vs. rescuing but also choosing a grey as my first bird. It is honestly so sad that bigger birds are shown as a "advanced/intermediate" level no bird is an easy pet. Don't expect everything to always be fun and easy, there are always rough patches. All parrots require a lot of time and engagement but are so worth the effort in the end. Every species has both advantages and disadvantages and no one bird is right for everyone. Parrots live a very long time, it doesn't make sense to get one bird just to help prepare you for another ( the one you have your heart set on) and also is not really fair for that bird. Please always do your research. However, don't let talk turn you away from the bird you want regardless of if you are a first time owner or not. I am so happy with my grey and I would have done the same thing looking back. It breaks my heart to think I could have compromised with smaller "beginner bird" and not have my baby Yoshi today.  Ill end this rant here although I could go on...lol Reflecting, I wish I would have had this perspective when I was deciding to get my first bird.

In 4 weeks Cody has managed to stop taking haldol medication for the first time in years and coco has opened up to men when before she would only interact with women. I’m so proud of the work I’ve done. They where both caged parrots and didn’t want to come out their cages for anything. Not food motivated so was tough. Now when I go in to their bedroom they literally bang their beaks on the cage to get out and come be where I am. It’s been one of the best experiences of my life and it’s very hard but the reward out weighs any amount of work. They would bite me and cause bleeding and bruising at the start. They really did not want anything to do with anything that wasn’t being alone in their cage. So for anyone worrying about this please don’t. If you are willing to PROVE To them you will care for them no matter what and no matter how much they bite and scratch you you’ll still love them and be kind to them I used to sit and eat dinner in front of their cage or I’d sit watching videos on the laptop on the floor and talk to them even tho they acted like they hated me. Now I’m their absolute everything. Please If u can help them and are willing to work they can open up and love again

text_content: I bought my current cockatoo when I was in my 20's, I turned 60 this year. I can attest to every detail that Clint mentioned. Every person that has met my bird over the decades has said they want one, and every single time I tell them "don't do it" then go on to explain many of the issues as Clint mentioned. If I had it to do again, as much as I love him, I wouldn't. Every aspect of my life has revolved around how to make it work with my bird. However, there are lonely birds in rescues, and lonely people who have time and love to give, that can make a good match. But a cockatoo (or any parrot) is not a good match for a younger person with many jobs, people, kids, and homes in their future.

video_title: Cockatoo, The Best Pet Dinosaur?
video_description: Cockatoos are fairly common pet parrots. There are some really good reasons for this. These are amazing birds! They are also ...
text_content:
I have a rescued Moluccan cockatoo, Kiah (Australian Aboriginal for "From the beautiful place."). I've had her since the Summer of '05. She was an import in the 60s, and was used as a breeding hen for the pet trade, and overbred to the point of bone and beak deformaties. When she was no longer of any use as a breeder, they dumped her, and because she was never socialized, she bounced from house to house. When I got her, nobody could go near her, because she would go ballistic. I did what I do with all my rescues; I set her cage up next to my computer, and fed her, watered her, and kept her cage clean, but other than that, I didn't look at her, talk to her, anything. It was about her observing me. Within two months, she made the decision to approach me and make first contact, and since then she's what we who work with toos call a "love sponge." She's a massive cuddler, will open her wings to give a hug (she was not taught this), and loves meeting most people.

That's the positive. She's also loud. For those curious, Moluccans are capable of being louder than the Umbrella Clint showed in the video. Moluccans are capable of screaming at 135dB, which is only 50dB louder than what can cause permanent hearing damage. They also stomp their feet when they are upset (okay, that's kind of adorable), which is almost always followed by screaming. Like many rescues, she's also a plucker/self mutilator, and wears a styling collar to keep the damage limited to only *looking* like she went through a blender, and not giving her the more damaging effects of having actually met the business end of her built-in one, also known as her beak. With the collar, she only does damage to her feathers; without it, she will tear into her skin. 

Basically, while I love my Kiah, and will have her until one of us croaks, I can say this with *total* confidence; you rated them high, Clint.

My aunt and uncle have a beautiful cockatoo! I desperately wanted one when I was younger, so I spent as much time with Bonita (yeah they don’t get points for name creativity😂❤) as I could!
Until I was put in charge of her for 2weeks when I was a teenager, while my aunt and uncle went on a cruise for their anniversary….
Bonita was chill for about the first 4-5 days (which was previously the longest time her “parents” had ever been gone and left me in charge of her before this cruise)— after that, all heII broke loose.  
She totally stopped eating(yes, she went on a hunger strike), she “screamed” almost nonstop and yelled for “mom/dad” (which was heartbreaking), she started destroying everything around the house that she could—and finally, she started ripping feathers out. 
After about 3 days of pure Bonita anarchy, they had to end their trip early and pay for a flight back home before the cruise was even over—because I simply couldn’t get Bonita to calm down, no matter what I tried. 

Sure as the sun rises, almost immediately after they get home—she starts eating normally, no longer shrieked all day and stopped destroying everything in sight!

I still love cockatoos (and Bonita) but I definitely reevaluated my desire for one after that. 😂❤
That was about 25 years ago and they still have never attempted to take another trip that long again, because they don’t want to risk another Bonita meltdown.

text_content:
Thank you for this video, especially your emphasis on how much responsibility and dedication these birds require to keep properly.

When I was a kid I was friends with someone whose parents owned a couple of cockatoos. The birds were very neglected and it made me incredibly sad. I never saw them leave their cages, and those cages were in a walk-in-closet sized room covered in bird dust, so they were extremely poorly socialized. The larger of the cockatoos would let me stroke him through his cage if I was careful, the smaller would hiss and attempt to bite if I came anywhere near her enclosure. Apparently the owners initially took better care of them, my friend told me when she was young the birds were frequently let out of their enclosures and would even accompany the owners on car rides around town (not the safest thing but at least the birds were getting attention and activity). I suspect the owners got tired of attending to the birds' needs after the novelty wore off or after the owners had children.

Animals are a commitment, not a decoration or a toy to be tossed aside when they get boring or when life gets stressful; people need to know what they're getting into and make sure they're capable of providing for an animal for its entire life, *especially* for animals that are high-needs. I am glad to see some other commenters here have changed their minds about getting a pet cockatoo, and I hope anyone who is still interested in owning one will understand how much time and work they'll need to put in to take proper care of these amazing birds.

text_content: I have 6 of them, all adopted. There is a reason why people get rid of them. Their snuggling is because they are not socialized the natural way, and they demand this every day. The mess, destruction, biting and the screaming is not for everyone. They are NOT beginner's pets. They are perfect for someone who is home all day, with nothing else to do but to cater to their cockatoo. Do not have neighbors - their decibel level is higher than any other parrot. And they live for a long time. They are also prone to plucking, and they can get prolapse, which is very expensive to treat - and many people (and other parrots) are allergic to the feather dust, which is very fine and gets into everything. Every new cage lasts a few weeks and then it needs new doors , latches or gates.They can pop locks and cage-bars and destroy your woodwork in minutes.

text_content: You know, this is great for me because once upon a long time ago, i worked at a dingy reptile shop that had a few birds. Two african greys, one scarlet macaw, and two umbrella cockatoos. One of which was named Tenguin. And tenguin, like all of the other birds there, was very neglected. He spent 97% of his time inside his dirty, empty, bird cage. He maybe had one toy in there at a time. Consequently, he was a very energetically frustrated bird that would constantly scream for attention because human interaction was the only time he got to do anything with all that energy. Peekaboo, singing songs, trading phrases, playing tag, getting treats, etc. Poor little guy all around. They all were, but i he took it the hardest. And his scream. Good god, his scream was so loud that it could be heard from the inside of the co-op, across a parking lot, and through the shut windows and doors of our building. It was so frequent, all the co op staff knew his name and could accurately identify him from inside their building solely by how loud his caw screetch was. I was 18 years old when i worked there. I spent a lot of time with tenguin trying to help entertain him because i didn't know what else i could do, he wasn't my bird and i knew nothing about birds. He ended up bonding with me during my time there. Well, the shop wasn't in a great financial situation in general, they had too many reptiles. So when covid hit they went under. I go back to visit a little before that finalizes, I'm 20ish right about then and next thing i know is I'm being sales pitch'd to buy Tenguin. 3k and I can have him and his gross ass cage! What a deal. Nevermind the fact that I was living in a rental room, had no space or knowlege about how to realistically care for birds. I almost considered buying him just to have the opportunity to give him to an avian rescue i knew of through my own exotics contacts. But 3k i didn't have to rehome a bird? That was the cruddy part. It sucks because i did genuinely love that bird, and in waaay different circumstances i might have took him on and figured it out as i went just to get him out of there, but honestly, just no. I don't know where he is now, and that sucks. I hope he finally found his way into better hands. But I dodged a BULLET.

text_content: So interesting story….I had only owned male cockatoos and when my best buddy B-bird passed I adopted a female bird who seemed to do the same thing.  The lady I adopted her from told me that she rescued this bird from people who were drug addicts and she thought the shaking was from withdrawal of cigarette smoke.  She said that she brought her to a vet and that was the vet’s conclusion 🙄 also. Well either the owner was dumb and the vet was being sarcastic? Or both her and the vet were dumb. Unfortunately she died as a result of an egg not forming due to a lack of calcium.  Her poop starting smelling awful and I told the vet what I thought was wrong with her. It was too late. $9,000 later she went into cardiac arrest and passed on. It really could have been avoided. My heart was shattered. 😊 So yes. Do not pet them when they’re hormonal and give your bird extra calcium during this time.  If they lay an egg feed the egg to the bird. It is full of vitamins and minerals. It will help your bird recover sooner as well.  Crush the shell into tiny tiny pieces or put the shell in a coffee grinder until it’s like a powder. I know it sounds gross but you need to replenish your little girl’s body immediately before she lays a second egg!!!❤

text_content: When I was in the Army, I was stationed on Okinawa, Japan. I was the barracks sergeant and had my own room, a pretty sweet deal. Walking through the town, I saw a small pet store and saw this beautiful white cockatoo I a cage there. Out of curiosity, I asked how much and the lady said $50 us dollars. I talked with my first segargent and he let me keep her in my room. We became very close. and I kept the top art of the cage open and made a perch from an old floor lamp. She became part of my life and woke me up in the mornings by flying over to the bed and pulling the blanket away from my face and grooming my eyebrows and lashes. I took her outside and let her fly around but she alway wanted to stay close to me and sit on my shoulder. When my tour was up I tried to take he back to the states but the animal quarantine restrictions were very strict and she would have had to stay in quarantine for a month by herself. I found a friend that was living on the island and gave her to him and his family a couple months before I left and visited her almost every day until she got used to them. I still think about here and wished I could have brought her home.

text_content: I am American and we adopted a cockatoo from a pet shop when she was 3 months old.  She hopped on my daddy's shoulder. So of course he had to bring her home!  We had her for 15 years.  My father - her primary owner got dementia and fell with her 2 times on his shoulder.  She got quite upset since she really loved my dad.  My mother and I also loved her and held her and played with her a lot.  She was only in her cage at bed time till morning.  My parents are in their 80's and I am 63 with a serious lung disease and stomach disease. It was getting hard to give her the quality of life care we believed she should have.  A wonderful young woman comes in twice month to clean for us for about 2 years.  Our bird took to her.  We gave her the bird, cage, all of our bird books, food and my mother made a notebook - how we fed her, human snacks (a couple times a week I would give her a very small piece of my pecan sandy cookie!), no-no foods, vet info, games we played, words she knows and the songs we sang to her and the one song she has learned 3 words to!  And the shop we got her from will trim her nails and wings for free. She is doing well.  It has been about 2 years and my daddy barely remembers her.  He named her after his well-loved grandmother Judy.  My momma and I still exchange memories of Judy.  And her 'new' owner shows us videos and tells about the new words she has learned and everything else wonderful she does!

text_content:
Cockatoos are bonkers, but I absolutely love them! I had a Goffin's I rescued in 2023 (she passed 6 months later and it broke my ❤), and I now have a Bare-eyed Cockatoo. My Bare-eyed was in the rescue for 2 years before he came home with me. I volunteer for the parrot rescue that he was at. After I'm done cleaning, feeding, changing water, etc at the rescue, you can always find me in the cockatoo room just sitting there and enjoying their company. As an introvert who loves my quiet/alone time and hates drama, everyone at the rescue is surprised that my favorite room there is the cockatoo room. It doesn't make sense to me either lol.😊
I think the only way I could handle owning an Umbrella is if I had no other pets and could devote all my time to it, which is not the case right now. For the time being, I'll just love on them at the rescue while they wait for their forever home. Please please, adopt don't shop for your next feathered friend!!!❤

video_title: Rescue Cockatoo Warns Wife To Stay Away From Husband With Evil Death Stare 😂🫵🏼 #cockatoos #foryou
text_content: our rescue Umbrella ONLY WANTS MY HUSBAND, when we sit together, he attacks me, and he is NOT FOOLING AROUND, unless you want to lose an ear. Some of these parrots choose their favorite and then become possessive and jealous over their pick. That is just how it is and there is no changing their mind. We drove from Maryland to NJ to get him, and that was about 5 years ago. I feed him, taught him new words, give him snacks, but when my husband shows up, I get out of the way, he goes right back to attacking me and biting me. He once came across an entire room to bite me. He is NOT going to change his mind. I understand this video 100%

text_content: My mom had a Malaccan cockatoo when I was a teen. He basically become mine, I'm the only one who would feed him, take care of him, play with him. My mom kicked me out at 16 for two years. I cam back and she told me stay out of the "bird room". I had to sneak in there and I found him almost starved to death, with his feathers ripped out. I convinced her to give him to a sanctuary while I secretly nursed him back to health. I still cry sometimes thinking about what it must have been like to live locked up in a cage for two years, starving. I wish people would stop buying birds if they're not going to give them all the attention they need.

text_content:
Many thanks to the caring couple who welcomed this feathered beauty into their lives!  Edgar will return all that love tenfold; you can tell he's a real sweetheart.  

When I saw the cage he was kept in for so long, my heart just broke for him; I cannot ever imagine keeping a bird in cage that small.  All parrots are incredibly intelligent, & cockatoos especially are mechanically inclined, so they require a great deal of mental stimulation (think unlock locks, unscrewing your doorknobs, & bolts & nuts; they love that kind of thing).  I'm so glad Edgar is with people who will love him, & give him the best life possible.  He may not be able to fly, but the love this family will share will make their hearts soar!

video_title: 10 Useless Facts About Cockatoos
video_description: You could call this a blooper reel, really. But we will call it 10 useless facts instead. Thanks to Mel Vincent for letting us use her ...
text_content: We have one we adopted , 22 year old male.   And we discovered he has a toll, you can pay in time spent around him,  or in blood.  The more blood you spill by holding him,  the faster he will come around to be your friend.   I'm his favorite and basically as since day 1 which was VERY odd,  he attached right to me I must look something like his former owner.  But randomly he would still just bite me as if he realized I was not who he thought.   But within a year we had a good relationship.   My wife and or any woman he seems to really REALLY hate, and has even jumped off his cage to attack my mother and mother in law.   As for my wife, she reduced the time cost by adding a little blood to it and they are also friends now. And it helps a lot to read the bird,   I made games out of everything he did, when he would get upset and start making high pitched scream sounds, I would lightly tap him on his back and make a " bock bock bock " sound,  and at some point that became a game, and he quit doing it.   Also Bobble Bobble, Kirby Coaster (flip him upside down and back) and others.   His screams we were able to replace with him barking.    We learned he hated the spray bottle we would use to mist him,  so we used it as threat not a bath and he would quit screming, but he replaced screaming himself with barking like our dogs.   So now he barks at things he don't like, and we are okay with that since its MUCH lower volume lol.

text_content:
As a parrot owner of 27 years - THANK YOU for giving the negative points to owning a parrot.  Everyone shows the cutesy, cuddly parts of life with a parrot, but rarely do they give the warnings.  I studied them for a year before I decided on a Congo African Grey.  I knew I would get bitten.  I knew they required time and attention.  I have many scars on my fingers, and one or two on my lips.  We love our boys, but we also understand them.  You HAVE to be able to read body language.  Greys are not as attention seeking a 'toos are, but they can be very aggressive during mating season - spring & fall.  They do not allow deviation from a set schedule, and they need a schedule.
Our orange-winged Amazon is very sweet and gentle.  The only time he bites is when there is a child in the house.  When a child enters, he no longer wants much to do with me.  He wants to cuddle with the child.  Otherwise he is patient, he is sweet - but when he screams - he makes your eardrums shake.  And he screams fairly often.
I really appreciate you putting it out there so people know what they are getting into.

video_description: Victoria was given six months to live. Rescued from a life of neglect, this cockatoo was weak, dehydrated, and silenced by years in ...
text_content: I have had my cockatoo (Socks) for 3 years and ever since that first night I have NEVER spent a night away from him. No more vacations, no more nights out with friends or weekends away. He is the first thing I give attention to when I wake in the morning, when I arrive home from work whether I am hungry, tired or need a bathroom break. He is the last thing I attend to before I go to bed and many times I just give in and allow him to sleep on the pillow next to me. I can be honest and say that sometimes it can be overwhelming but I know he is my responsibility. I am his human and he is emotionally attached to me. I am 57 years old and it is a fact that he will very likely out live me and I have made provisions for that in my will. Before getting him I was told that cockatoos love hard but I did not fully comprehend this. If you are not capable of giving these beautiful beings your love and attention then please do not get one. They are not pets, they are companions and a part of your family.

video_title: Scarlet Macaw, The Best Pet Parrot?
video_description: Scarlet Macaws are pretty much the mascot for parrots, if parrots were a sports team. They are incredibly iconic, tropical looking, ...
text_content:
The first parrots I ever fell in love with were a hyacinth named Puni and a scarlet named Celeste. They were such sweet birds. We were buddies when I was lucky enough to hang out with them, and I've been wanting one since I met them.

Saying that, their person was a VERY experienced parrot owner whose life revolved around his birds. Seeing how much care and time they required helped me understand that no matter how much I loved them, it's hard to give them a good life, and it's cruel to take one on if you're not prepared. They're less like dogs or cats, and more like having a flying, screaming toddler with bolt cutters attached to their faces.

text_content:
Thank you for doing this. Educating people about not impulse buying is so important because so many kids can watch things and get this wistful image of the perfect pet in a perfect world.

I know I have a personal dream to one day be able to live a relatively good life that's perfect for raising a Mollucan Cockatoo. I've done a lot of research and I know full well what it takes to take care of even the simplest of parrots - and trust me. There is no simple parrot.

You have to devote a lot of time, money, and love into them. Most, if not all, parrots are as intelligent as a 3 year old child. A toddler! And they live upwards of 20 to 80 years on average, depending on size and species. They're lifetime commitments. You'll literally be raising a toddler for a majority of your living life. While that can be rewarding for those who know what they're up against and are prepared for that, then they're perfect additions to the family. But you should never impulse buy any animal. Ever. You should always do your research and prepare, be it a mouse, a dog, a parrot, or a horse. Always make sure you can actually PROVIDE before you OWN.

text_content:
As a owner of a parrot for 12 years:
1.) Macaws are the LOUDEST parrots in the rainforest. So they will scream EXTREMELY LOUD when they play, want attention, are upset, when they want your food, ect.
2.) Their intelligence is the equivalent to a human toddler. They need as much attention, love, & play as a human toddler. If you don't give them this time they will have significant mental health & physical healthy problems.
3.) They go through puberty just like humans. Once the reach puberty (in their early teens) their personality changes. They will become more aggressive, jealous if u hangout with anyone else, and randomly biting. 
4.) They poop on EVERYTHING constantly. They poop every 15 minutes so you have to be ok with them pooping on ur furniture, nice house items, & with deep cleaning the cage every couple of days.
5.) Vet bills are EXTREMELY EXPENSIVE. The average vet bill for me was $400. When she became sick it was around $1,000 each vet visit for mediations & tests.
6.) Their average lifespan in captivity is the human life span. If something happens to you or if u adopt a young macaw they will out live you. They have SEVERE PTSD if they have to switch owners because of the deep emotional connection. This can lead to self mutilation.
7.) They love only 1 human at a time. They will be jealous & hate any other humans you are close to.

text_content: I am a disabled combat vet,  most of my buddies are married with kids and have jobs, other responsibilities. So I do consulting work from home. Basically, I have all the time in the world. Not married. No kids. When I was a kid, I went on vacation to Colombia. They had two dogs, a cat, two, parrots, and a blue and yellow macaw. The moment I saw it sitting on his perch outside, that was it. Not a day went by were we did not see each other. I fed her, I cuddled her, showered her. The other green parrots were so jealous, even when i played with them too, they just could not be on my shoulders at the same time. They went at it like an avian UFC. I spoiled the heck out of her, which was a big mistake. A few days before I left, the owners, rightfully, put her in an island next to the hotel to distant me from her. I kid you not. She would call out my name. We knew the owners of the establishment, and sadly she died not long after I left. For anybody thinking about getting one of these beauties they can get incredibly attached to their owners. Thinking about getting one since ill be able to take care of it. Money and time is not really an issue. I do have nice furniture, a 65" OLED 4K TV, which ill have to cover it with a plexiglass enclosure. But i really want a companion. I LOVE macaws. They are a work of art. I will give it a good, caring home. I recently relocated to Australia, if anybody is a breeder or is a temp care. Hit me up. i will travel anywhere within the country.

text_content: Wonderful video on how to be a great friend to these awesome birds. I am a board member for Exotic Bird Rescue of Oregon (USA) and am blessed with a loving Catalina Macaw myself. People, please listen very closely to this message. It is wonderfully put. On average, exotic birds are re-homed, more than 5 times before finally finding a 'forever' home. and the effects on them can be horrendous. Everything from self-mutilation (feather plucking and even tearing holes in their own flesh) to nearly un-controllable screaming and aggression. (Nearly...  a lot of work and love can remedy a lot of problems).  Also, find a bird club in your area and get some experience with them. Learning to read body language can save the bird and YOU a lot of blood and tears.  I only have one... small... disagreement, and that is with clipping wings and free flighting. I agree that clipping wings seem a travesty but there are situations where, for the safety of the bird, I would recommend it. Also being part of a rescue I can assure you that even well-trained birds get scared and fly away on occasion... sometimes never to be seen (alive at least) again. We get several calls a week regarding birds that 'have never flown off before' having been scared and are gone....it is heartbreaking. If you do take your bird outside....please PLEASE think about an appropriate flight harness.

text_content:
We just rescued a blue and gold Macaw named Roscoe. We were told he we *roughly* 14 years old, but the people we got him from said they only had him for four years, so they're aren't even sure. 

He had quite an attachment to me very quick, which is crazy because I've never been a bird owner before until I met my Fiancée, who hand raised and sold birds as a teenager and has dealt with them her whole life. 

But he is very territorial and will bark and scream at us if we get too close or hug and kiss on one another. And if I'm not in the room with her, he will snap at her and try to bite, but never does that to me. She hasn't done anything to him, and I haven't done anything special to deserve his undying love and affection. So i was curious if that seems to be typical of them. 

For some experience context for myself; She had a cockatiel when we got together, named Blaiz, and we rescued a blind and arthritic orange winged amazon parrot shortly after we got together, named Peter. Both alive and as well as can be. 
Blaiz is now 15, cranky, old boy, but he belonged to her son, who unfortunately passed away a few months back, so there is 0 chance we get rid of him. 

Peter is 43. He was in a home that didn't care about him. His original owner passed away a few years back from old age, and the lady we got him from didn't want him, nor did she take care of him. His beak was so overgrown that it's now permanently shifted his jaw so his mouth is crooked. And his talons were so overgrown that he couldn't walk on his feet, to which one grew very stiff, and now he can't open it all the way. (Vet said there was nothing we could do about it because it had progressed too far for medicine to be of any use.)

Roscoe is very healthy, aside from the awful diet the last couple had him on, which consisted of peanuts, lots of fried potatoes, and paper. 🙃

We have fixed the diet. He now has a regular diet of fresh fruits and veggies as he should, with a nutriberri blend that we mix plumage food into. And the occasional peanut or pistachio for a treat. 

He's a tricky little guy, but he is a huge sweetheart. He has an attitude, but he is much larger than Peter, so while he has never attacked me, (he snapped at me once, but didn't hurt or even leave a mark, so I'm sure he was just playing) I'm still pretty nervous around him. He let's me pick him up, and pet his head. I haven't tried his face around his eyes yet, so I'm going to try that today and see if he's okay with it. 

I was just wondering if you had any tips for helping him adjust to her? She's always been a huge bird lover, and she was so excited to get Roscoe. He was not very nice to her the first week we had him, and she got very frustrated by him, and actually yelled (her yells aren't very yelly) at him. Blaiz is actually a total asshole because her son didn't really take care of him, and I've seen Blaiz bite her and draw blood and she's never gotten mad enough to yell at him. So I can tell that she's really upset by his behavior, more so because he is so nice to me. I just want to do my best to help him adjust to her, too. 

I'm sorry that was a lot of stuff to say. 😅

text_content:
Ever since I was a child, I dreamed of parrots and prairie dogs. I've been living with prairie dogs since 1997. But you have to create a special habitat and design one that can be transported on vacation. 
Living with parrots must be much more difficult: you can't hide them in your T-shirt to go to the movies; it's impossible to get into a hotel room with 5 animals hidden on top of my wife and me; 1 parrot makes a lot more noise than a whole family of prairie dogs.
Still, I love these magnificent birds. The black palm cockatoo is truly magnificent. The Hyacinth macaw was the bird of my dreams, and I still find it so splendid.

text_content:
I have a very large beautiful male yellow nape  named Cricket.  I have had him for 27 years (he is 28) and I got him from a sanctuary where he'd been placed after being abused.  I think HR is the perfect parrot, but it took a lot of time and work to gain his trust, and he still barely tolerates men.  

He is relatively quiet, and sing and talks and doesn't scream except rarely and only if he senses danger like a hawk or helicopter or cat.and is incredibly loving in his way and extremely affectionate.  I am disabled an have several chronic illnesses including brittle diabetes, and he learned very early to alert me to low blood sugar emergencies by rubbing vigorously on my cheek and saying "it's OK!"  "You alright?" and not stopping until I ate or drank (of course he got jackpot treats!). If it was late or bedtime he would often not go  in his cage, but run to the bedroom and station himself on the headboard of the bed and would want to watch me all night. Getting him in his cage was hard on those nights!   I live in NYC and he is a fixture in the neighborhood,  If he isn't seen for a  few days during good weather,  people come and ask about him.  He constantly flirts with the neighborhood women calling them to. "come see the birdie", and telling them that they're beautiful. His only flaw is that he's messy,but I wouldn't change a thing about him.  He is a true blessing in my life,  and he also brings smiles to almost everyone who sees him!

text_content: I have a Blue Front Amazon.  He hardly talks at all, he is actually very quiet, and wants to murder everyone but me.  I think it is very important that people consider their expectations when getting a bird, because there are NO guarantees - especially when it comes to talking.  I know people with African Grey parrots who are not good talkers.  You never know what kind of bird you are going to wind up with.  Before I got mine, I considered all of my reasons for wanting a parrot, and decided that I would accept him for who he was and would be committed to him for as long as he lives.  Sure, it would be cool if he talked more, but I don’t love him any less because he doesn’t.

video_description: The Peach Fronted Conure, often confused as the Orange Fronted Conure, is a slightly lesser known Conure in the pet trade.
text_content:
The Hardiest bird ever....I give it 5 out of 5.

Your scores all air on cautionary sides.....mine is 18yrs old and I live in Wales...temperature changes are dramatic.

This bird is like owning a dog, its attention demanding for sure, and it needs it.

The Peach is the king of conures, very smart, very reading...its not the most playful bird, it's more reserved than most conures, but they do like attention.

They display their mood very expressively through their body , wing raises, wing stretches , hisses and chest bump attacks.....but all, are mainly bravado. It's a forgiving bird.....but still susceptible to it's natural instincts on occasion.

Under scored heavily i reckon

video_title: Why Parrots May Bond to One Person (And What You Can Do About It)
video_description: A viewer recently asked me about their Orange Wing Amazon parrot who only allows one person (the owner) to pet or touch him.
text_content:
First off, i wish you were more north so we could grab dinner but wanted to share my piece also i have 5 birds, two green cheeks, two quakers and one lovebird. Both quakers and my second conure were given up and i worked at a bird store at the time (I dont really condone purchasing birds anymore because of actually seeing it, but that's another story). The first conure i brought home was about six months old, now about 3.5 years, its crazy how time passes and she is truly my spirit animal, understands me more than any person. My lovebird never really truly bonded with me but does step up. My green quaker is older at about 10 years old, does not fly, and has only bonded with a family member but it's okay. My blue quaker is pretty sociable with everybody and is definitely the most playful..

And here's my problem child,  my pineapple conure was a rehome and he's about estimated 6/7 years old.. i do not really know his previous situation which i know can complicate things. But he does not like hands at all,  he will hang out on my shoulder and does preen me, never have gotten bitten by him ever. But anybody else, he still does not like hands but he actually really goes to attack anybody else. All this to say i know he does trust me and if i approach very slowly he will let me pet his neck, but very infrequently.. any suggestions on how to work with him to be more acceptable with handling/ hands?

text_content:
I "rescued" my RES named Dip from a severely neglected pond back when i was 12 years old and she was the size of a silver dollar (I totally didnt know it was bad to take animals out of the wild at the time, but i did know she was invasive and was probably going to die if left in that disgrace of a pond). She's been my roommate ever since and is creeping up to 18 years old soon!

Easily one of my most sociable pets, and demands head/shell scritches when i walk past her. Dip will cause as much noise as possible with her shell and claws on the decor and side of the tank until I pet her, only after sufficient shell scritches and head rubs will she calm down.

They sure are a lot of maintenance compared to other reptiles (so.many.water changes.), but she has definitely been worth it

text_content: I’ve had my EBT for 30yrs now. I was 5yrs old in ‘91 when I found her wandering through our milk parlor on our dairy farm of all places & since at age 5 I was already in love with TMNT I picked her  up bc I didn’t want her to get stepped on by a cow first of all but also bc I wanted a pet turtle lol. She’s never been a shy or scared turtle at all, in fact she’s never completely “boxed up” on me that I can ever remember. Idk for sure an age and my vet isn’t totally sure either but I did learn she was a she when we first took her to a vet when I first found her. I built a big “tortoise table” for her about 17-18yrs ago when I was at college & took her with me to my apartment and she’s loved it ever since. I have a substrate side for her on 1 end with both a heat pad & light, in the middle I transitioned to a small soil area where there’s ferns, dandelions, & lilacs that she eats right out of the ground as the grow with the help of a light & misting system and then on the far side I have a sloping transition to a basking pool. Her table is 8ftlong by 3ft wide with 6inch sides around the whole frame so she doesn’t see through, walk off and hurt or kill herself by cracking or shattering her shell. This is a really great video for anyone who has an EBT they wanna improve their husbandry skills with or are thinking of getting one & doing their due diligence beforehand which I highly recommend with ANY animal you plan on getting as a pet!

It broke my heart but I had become disabled and had no choice.  It was worse than having a child leave me with an empty nest.

video_title: My Box Turtle Care Guide! How I Take Care Of My Oldest Pet (Learn From My Mistakes)
video_description: My Box Turtle Franklin is the oldest pet I have! At 22 years old I've been caring for him the majority of my life and have learned a ...
text_content: I have a Desert Box Turtle named Diego. She showed up to my back porch full grown with dog chew holes on her shell about 25 years ago and we've been best friends ever since. She's my childhood pet and I still have her living in my room for winter with all her lights in a 75 Gallon Tank. I move her tank to an outside enclosed porch around April because she hates refrigerated air and bring her back inside the first week of October. I never let her play outside alone unless I'm with her because I don't want to lose her. She's about the size of a McDonalds Cheeseburger. I have to admit that when I first got her I had no idea what I was doing, or if she had a family that she never returned to and I feel bad about that but now I'm commited to her because we moved over a decade ago and she came with. I have no idea how she got into the yard because the yard was completely fenced off and there were no holes under the fence. She just appeared at the back door. Now that she's getting older she's very active and happy and healthy but I can't get her to eat anything other than bananas (with bird vitamins 2x a week) and hamburger meat. She loves those but will absolutely not accept any other food. I feed her every day even if she doesn't eat but it's offered. I take her for a yearly check-up every beginning of October at the veterinarian and the doctor says she looks healthy. We're not sure how old she is but she was full grown when she joined my family. I'm Autistic and don't have any children or a wife of my own and I wonder if my childhood beloved pet is going to outlive me. What's she going to do if I die before her? I feel that releasing her into the wild (especially after decades in captivity and in a totally different location) is unthinkable after being in captivity for so long and having her needs taken care of but what's she going to do if I die? I wonder if there's any rescues in New Mexico. Hopefully I don't die anytime soon but she just very well may outlive me. That's a problem.

text_content: I'm holding my box turtle right now! I've had her for 35 YEARS!  When my nephew was seven, he found and kept her. She was sick and wouldn't eat, so I nursed her back to health and asked if I could have her. Box turtles are a big responsibility. Temperature is very important. If they don't like their conditions, they go on hunger strikes, get weak and die! They need exercise, interaction, and a big aquarium. They are expensive! Canned food, eye drops, shell lotion, heat lamps, reptile bark, special bulbs, big aquariums, vet bills ,etc.  So leave them in the wild or you might have a dead turtle and a sad kid.                                                          That being said ...I love my turtle, and she loves me. She fusses for me to hold her. Plays tug of war and bite the beard.  Likes classic rock music, swims in a big pot for exercise on bath day. I try to hold her for a few hrs every day. Her name is'' Cordelia'' but mainly call her ''Turtle Pants''. A fun pet but not an easy one!                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    PS. .... If you already have a box turtle, I recommend Zoomed box turtle canned food. It's about $3.50 a can (4 feedings) and a steady feeding schedule. My turtle eats every 2 1/2 days.  Mine won't eat dry food. Lettuce doesn't have enough nutrition and bananas is like giving them candy every day. Special box turtle food has fruits, vegetables, fish meal, vitamins, proteins etc.  Also, a bulb that gives both UVA and UVB rays about $30 to $60. Bulbs give them vitamins, so does the eye drops and lotion.  also a aquarium thermometer. and carefully trim the beak and toenails.

text_content: I had a snapping turtle that was rescued from our parking lot on a hot summer day as a fresh hatchling, about the size of a small coin.  I raised her and handled her every day for years.  Her and I would go all over.  She got so used to people that she was like a puppy, with Petsmart being one of her favorite places.  Usually trying to climb on my shoulder and then from a shopping cart (with padding) when she got too big, trying to get a look at everything.  I had so much trust in her that even when she was big I would try to give her as much free roam time around the house as she wanted.  She usually ended up exploring some then coming back to me, try and dig into my side a bit and fall asleep.  Saying that though, I would never recommend one as a pet to anybody.  Mine was a sweety but it took me years of spending hours with her every day for her to become tame.  And all it takes is 1 bite to mess you up pretty bad for life.

text_content:
This specific video came up when i  searched for turtle care. My boyfriend and his family are very ... very ... particular about his turtle. Hes had it since he was a small child and his parents wanted it more than he did, but they also ... dont have much in there. 

Hes been in only water for mostly his entire life. No basking, no decor in the tank, nothing more to eat than the dry food. I bought him some gourmet food like two years ago along with a large rock decor that lets him at least dry out. Its been super hard to try and add things to the tank. Everytime i buy something to add i feel like ive insulted his parents. They try and convince me he needs nothing more than he has and hes been fine for years, and also even tried to get me to return the items i bought but i threw out the reciept so they couldnt. 

 At least he has a massive tank, and a decent filtration system. But im the only one who throws lettuce in for a treat and i feel like everything i try, makes them look at me a certain way. Though, maybe i should be harsher with them about it, i know if this relationship continues to moving in together ill get to take care of the turtle and i look forward to the day in all honesty. Im doing more research right now to see what else i can add to the tank to help the guy, and ill just keep it up. Its hard to convince someone theyve been mistreating a reptile when they dont believe they have been at all since "he seems fine." 

This turned into a rant, but i did want to let you know most of all that for all the videos of yours ive never seen, this will be the first, but not the last. Thank you. You affect the lives of many a turtle in a tank, and i want you to know you will be affecting this one.

text_content:
This was an amazing introduction to the different types of tortoises and turtles out there, but also because you talk about responsibility in the first few minutes! I own a Russian (Morty the Tortie) and a Red-footed (Yurtle the Turtle) and respect them both so much as amazing reptiles! Russians don't need just a clear enclosure to not understand boundaries. Our Morty is in a dark enclosure and when he wants to get out for a walk, will repeatedly bang, scratch, and try to climb out. One thing I think you could have hit on with the Russian is that they need lots of exercise and/or a nice big enclosure, because in the wild they impressively walk 4 MILES A DAY (pretty impressive when you take their size into account!) Their also speed demons! My guy can run off in a flash, so you have to be careful to not lose sight of them. 

A big pro is their amazing personalities! Morty the Tortie reminds me of a grumpy old man who is actually lovable under their hard shell! He's very social and so smart! They're amazingly great pets, but just like you, I wish pet stores would not sell wild caught and teach buyers that they need a big enclosurer of about 6 square feet. 

Thanks so much for teaching responsibility in reptile ownership, something rare for channels because they are also breeding them. Also, I LOVE that you spoke about rescuing sulcatas. Amazing video! I subscribed instantly!

text_content: We had one growing up, but not for the typical reason. My dad was offered a RES that had a 4 inch shell size from someone who didn't want it anymore. He set up a tank in his own personal office and meticulously cared for it for years until it grew too big to be comfortable for it. He then dug a pond in the back yard for it, which it stayed in for another decade, well cared for. After he'd had it for at least twenty years he had a local biology teacher offer a very good setup for it (I believe the carapace was 8-9 inches though at that point) with the promise of giving it back should they no longer have a home for it. I believe it is still alive at almost 30 years right now!

video_description: The red eared slider (Trachemys scripta elegans) is probably the most popular aquatic turtle available today. They are beautiful ...
text_content: I got my Red Eared Slider Turbo when she was approx 1-2 years old, from a cousin who didn't want her anymore. That's her, my icon picture. She had to have an x-ray. I had no idea what I was in for, but I wouldn't trade her for the world. She's been with me now for 17 years, most of my life. She lives in a 110-gallon Tuff-Tub, for which I have custom-built a landing platform and heat and light rig for her. Full-spectrum + UVB + ceramic heat emitter + a canister filter rated for a 500-gallon outdoor pond. Here's what they don't tell you: when you have a turtle this big they don't make habitat supplies for you. You have to get very comfortable thinking outside the box, breaking out the power tools, and making late-night trips to your local hardware store sometimes. But I love my baby! She's smart, stubborn, the vet always complements her on her beautiful carapace, and she's afraid of precisely nothing. She's the perfect fit for me as a companion animal.

text_content: I beg your pardon, “ALL” turtle owners get the lighting and tank size, and food wrong,?!? Might want to reword speech! And I’m ALSO on a budget and in between jobs right now. BUT, my babies Elsa and Olaf are my priority! Even before my son!!(Calm down everyone who’s ready to say that’s awful, my boy is 23!)!! They have a 115ltr tank, fluval filter, constant water temp at a comfortable 80dgs, 3 basking stations, it’s one in rock, another in grass and the 3rd in wood., all with ramps and slides and various other things to hide/play with, have the run of my living room to come and go as they please. I actually can’t get a minute seat by myself cos my gorgeous girl is up my leg, and burried into me, whilst my beautiful boy wrecks anything he can’t get over, under or through!! A 3ft uva/uvb lamp, and in winter, I sleep on my sofa with them cuddled up beside me, and OH, on their VELVET heat mat. They also LOVE a swim in the big tub whist cleaning them out. Their diet consists of pellets and vitamin drops, tuna, king prawns, chicken, greens, tomatoes, strawberries etc!! The only thing I fail to do is use Evian or San Pellegrino for the jacuzzi effect, in the tank, my bad!! I am however in a country with the best fresh and clean water in the world. So, sir, please do not sum up as a whole, try individuals. I LOVE my babies so much, even I myself know it’s ridiculous! text_content: I love my russian Walter! He is the craziest little tortoise ever! With Russians you can actually use the vertical space since they LOVE to climb! I have a second floor in the indoor enclosure and he loves it. I also found very easy to manage the humidity by lining the bottom with plastic tarp and actually planting the furniture plants in the substrate, they need to be watered often so they help maintain the humidity in the ground where they want to borrow (although don’t get too attached to the plants he/she will must likely try to climb it) We also have a huge yard where he goes a few times a week and he exercises going all over the place several times, this gives him the exercise he needs and direct sunlight. Mazuri pellets for the diet with fresh greenies, the flowers of the season and a calcium block at all times. Change the greenies often and include some red, orange, or yellow veggies like squash, pumpkin, carrots but give just a few times a month, this gives them important nutrients missing in veggies of other colors.

video_title: RUSSIAN TORTOISES ARE THE BEST BEGINNER PET REPTILE!
video_description: Guys! I did it! I found the best beginner reptile! Let me explain my reasoning as to why a Russian Tortoise is the best for beginners ...
text_content:
Wholeheartedly agree on your point about having to be passionate about keeping any reptile. A dog is easy, you just have to provide them with one of thousands of good food products that are readily available in any supermarket, a place to sleep, a daily walk and to train them in basic things like not going to the toilet indoors - you have to be particularly useless to be unable to meet their basic needs. Horsefield/Russian Tortoises don't require the same amount of attention as a dog and are far cheaper to care for, but they're still far more complex in terms of their basic needs and a lot of people don't do their research before buying them.

The unfortunate thing with their popularity as a "beginner reptile" and how easily available they are in comparison with other reptiles is that a lot of the time parents get them for their kids as a first pet thinking they're on a similar level to a hamster or guinea pig in terms of care, they don't realise how much work actually goes into the husbandry and often the kids get bored of them within a year and want a dog so you end up finding hundreds of poorly looked after tortoises being sold online that have health issues like MBD from lack of UVB, poor diet and being kept on improper substrate and they commonly perish at a far too young age from more serious problems like respiratory issues or even just stress from not having their basic needs met. The shop were I got my first one from didn't even give any information on how to care for them or ask if we had the right set up, I just walked in, pointed at the one I wanted, paid and walked out of the shop with it - luckily I'd already done my research, but I do worry about how many people do just walk in there and buy one on a whim without knowing a single thing about them. Whenever I've spoken to someone who's thinking about getting a tortoise I always recommend that they get one from a dedicated reptile shop or a trusted breeder who won't sell one to you unless you can prove you have the right set up and will provide you with a care sheet.

video_title: Sulcata tortoise setup
text_content: If you're looking for a cheap outside enclosure when he gets a little bigger. I bought a 19ft longx10ft wide and 6.5ft high chicken enclosure on Amazon for 220$. I'm expanding my 13ftx10ft one I already have for my male Russian tortoise. I adopted him from someone keeping him in a tiny tank so l wanted to spoil him. I put a lot of edible plants and weeds in using the tortoise table app. Added some hides, an underground tunnel, some hills, and a hamster wheel he surprising loves. Watching him enjoy it is beyond worth it and it's really not as much work as you'd think. I started with the basics, making sure the enclosure was escape and predator proof. Added a few plants and a hide. Then just kept adding from there. I also let my bearded dragon outside in it during the day and leopard gecko for a few hours at night.

There is a balance between handling, socializing and stressing a snake out. I’m no biologist but as a keeper everything  he has said in this video is textbook how to raise a snake. Hard work? Oh what timing, been for the past several days going deep into reptile research, and last night and today been talking with a friend about ball python morphs/morph combos we really want! text_content:
Beautiful burm!  I love Burmese pythons. Its a shame that what people see on the news about Florida is all that most people will ever know about them. 

Theyve been a favorite of mine since i was a kid. Long story short, I wanted an African rock python for my 10th birthday (i had been keeping ball pythons and other snakes for years) but got a Burmese python instead. 

Now im in my mid 30s and keep Retics and Afrocks too. They are all so different but i love them all. 

Back in the day Burms were easily the most popular of the 3, but holy cow have Retics taken over lately! Hardly anyone keeps Burms and even fewer keep Afrocks these days.

 IMO Burms are the most practical for most people. Although they are more prone to respiratory issues. 
Afrocks are hardier for sure, but require a better understanding of snake behavior when it comes to handling them. They are NOT mean, but they are less forgiving than burms or retics on average. If you put the time in though, they are the most interesting to work with by far. Even smarter than retics believe it or not! more powerful and athletic too! At least lb for lb.

text_content:
Florida just banned tegus and iguanas, my life is sad now.

Supposedly they might make an exception if you catch a wild one from Florida, but at the moment it seems like they, along with all the large constrictors and Nile monitor are completely banned (with the exemption of keepers with tagged animals and unrealistic cage requirements, and zoos they are completely illegal now).

R.I.P. my dreams of a pet tegu.

PS: I understand how problematic the species that are invasive are, I live in Florida and have seen Burmese pythons and many (like thousands) of green iguanas in the wild and have seen the problems they cause. But banning them isn’t going to make all of these reptiles disappear, let’s just hope this doesn’t spread to other states or countries.

text_content: I had one of these in high school in the '70's.  I researched these as best I could before buying it, even to the point that I learned V. salvator was far superior to V. niloticus as pets.  Visited a biker in Fla. who had a 6 footer that he kept like a dog in an indoor kennel...spiked collar and all.  His lizard would come when called like a dog, and seemingly enjoyed being petted like a dog.  After that, I was hooked, and bought one a little bigger than the one in the video.  My dad built his enclosure.  He was literally like a cross between a dog, a cat and a dragon.  I enjoyed being his buddy for several years before he developed a respiratory infection.  In force-feeding him his medicine for that, I traumatized his mouth, resulting in his getting mouth rot which proved fatal.  This video brought tears to my eyes, remembering both Godzilla and my dad who helped me care for him.  Thank you.

video_title: Savannah Monitor, The Best Pet Lizard?
video_description: The savannah monitor (Varanus exanthematicus) is one of the coolest animals on planet Earth, but does that make it a good pet?
text_content:
Absolutely love this video. Wish it came with every half assed care sheet pet stores hand off for these guys. Wouldn't give mine up for the world, but they (as many other monitors) are not what most people tend to expect with a reptile. All my monitors have rooms, Colonies of food and deep substrate. I gotta tell ya, the cost of the correct substrate as well as the cost of all the larger roach species (well over 1000$ for 8 species of medium and large roaches alone) in the past few months from private breeders, cheaper that way) for food SEVERELY outweighed the cost of converting a bedroom, vet checkups, and continuous electricity for lighting plus the lighting itself altogether. 

Almost all of my monitors, and my Argentine tegu, are adoptees/rescues from people. People who had them in a breeder (40g) tank when they were at least 2 feet long, or loving families who just couldn't take care of them anymore due to either health or housing problems (how I got my Savannahs and my tegu). They were incredibly aggressive, mean, and dangerous animals. But you give them the space they deserve, and the respect they absolutely deserve- they come around so fast it's amazing. My Nile monitor, I got him from someone who said he does nothing but bite and escape. He has the largest room in my enclosure, and does have his days where he doesn't really want to deal with me, but he immediately lost his aggression- he grew curious of me rather than fearful of me. You work on their terms, you give them respect, and in return they give you coexistence. 

Not a lovely dovey animal, definitely dangerous animals  if your not aware of how to interact with them, but incredibly intelligent and absolutely amazing to live with and I love them more than anything in the world. 

Ps. Your also correct, my arms are always scratched to hell. I do have leather handling gloves and cut proof sleeves from work (I work with razor sharp sheet metal, but every cut on me is from one of my animals) but I'm either too lazy or too forgetful to ever put them on. Lucky me I have a high pain tolerance, and but man they make you bleed a LOT.

video_description: I Don't Like Savannah Monitors as Pets #savannahmonitors #reptiles.
text_content:
Mine is 3, got him at 2 weeks old from a store in Florida, paid 45 bucks for him, he was a sweetheart in the beginning, 3 months in he DISPICED MY GUTS, BIT, WHIPPED AND HISSED 100% of the time I tried some contact, did water bonding floating him in lukewarm
Water and my hand as the only island to grasp to, that made him understand he could trust me, he made a 180 after 8 or so sessions of water bonding, now he’s still a cranky dude, here and there he pushes me out of his terrarium like a bull, head on, but never ever more biting, Whipping or hissing, I love this lizard, it was a real trust bond and this made it much more personal and truly bonding with your companion is way better in my opinion than buying a boring gecko and playing with it once or twice a week…

video_title: Beginner Poison Dart Frogs - Species Overview and Selection Guide
video_description: Choosing your first dart frog species can be a very difficult task, but today's video will hopefully help you narrow down your choice.
text_content:
Excellent video. I keep a bunch of the ones you listed, Amizonica being my first. I now have Oyapock, Azureus, Vanzolinii, Serensis, Fantastica monte cristo, Epipedobates anthonyi, and Opphaga pumilio punta laurel. The boldest are definitely the Oyapock and Azureus, but the Anthonyi are also incredibly bold. The fantastica is by far the least bold, often I will go 6 or 8 weeks without seeing her. the Serensis is similar but I hear him every morning. when I first got into frog keeping I bought frogs based on their beautiful coloration, but after a few years of keeping I started to buy them more based on their calls. the Oophaga pumilio probably has my favorite call, but the Vanzolinii, Serensis, and Leucamelas have incredible calls too. I wake up daily to them. The Anthonyi call more than all the other frogs combined and also have a very beautiful call. 

I would agree with everything you said here. You just make me wish I could have some phyllobates, unfortunately the entire genus is prohibited where I live for some insane reason I can't understand. 

Interestingly I keep my anthonyi in a very unconventional way. They share a very large paludarium with an emerald tree boa. I am very aware that darts can and readily do drown so a paludarium is not typically suggested but these ones seem to do well in there. The babies have never known any other environment having come out of the water in that tank. The tree boa is fully nocturnal and fully arboreal where the species of darts are quite terrestrial and diurnal. The tank is 48"x48"x18" or 122x122x45 cm with about 11 gallons of water which houses some neon tetras.

text_content:
My U2 is not sure what to make of the sounds coming from my iPad right now 😂

My baby came to us back in 2014. We were his 3rd home in 4yrs…because he is loud 😢. He was sooooo afraid of balls because they had been thrown at his cage when he screamed. Afraid of anything moving too fast around him because someone hit his cage….for being a cockatoo and being loud. We love him so much and I cannot imaging having a quiet home…and can never live in an apartment 😂😂😂😂. My oldest…his true human,  will take him when I am too old to care for him. 💖

I am single in large part because when someone walks into my home and my birds (I also have an Amazon) start talking to you and you don’t respond….yeah, that tells me all I need to know. 🤷‍♀️ Sydney has been with my family longer than anyone else in his life and I feel so blessed that he loves us.

video_description: I Adopted a Cockatoo from a Rescue — Now He Sings to Me Every Day ❤️ ❤️ This is the story of a Cockatoo who refused to ...
text_content: I’ve lived nearly 40 years of my life with up to 4 parrots. I discovered a CH conure in a feed store. He was untamed and open banded. It was 1988 and quarantine stations were still legal in California at the time. He had been trying to take baths in the back of the fish tanks. The lady who owned the feed store was a friend and she wanted a good home for him so she gave me a cage and all the supplies for $35 and gave me the bird. She called it a promotion. She just fell in love with him and even though she didn’t want to keep him she knew I would give him the best home. It was a challenge as I had never hand tamed a wild parrot that had never been handled. Luckily there was a robust parrot companion community and I learned how to tame him. My spouse was active duty military at the time and so he was out on a ship at sea in the West Pacific, I had nothing to do in my spare time when I wasn’t at work. My new conure had a pretty portable standard pacific parrot cage. I took him with me whenever I went house sitting or visiting my uncles place in Huntington Beach. So my parrots have always been members of the family and not just exotic pet wallpaper to impress people with. A decades long relationship with a parrot has its ups and downs but as the years go by, the closeness is really priceless. I find the more time we spend together the closer our bond gets. Are there times you get frustrated with them? Of course but I think if you understand how your kind of bird thinks and behavior can be put into that context, you can start to really unlock the key to providing a rewarding life for both you and your birds. I’ve a lot of experience with parrots but I still don’t feel qualified to adopt a cockatoo. They need to be the center of attention and from what I’ve seen especially with Moluccan’s they have a lot of energy. They are so special though just absolutely adorable.

video_title: Parrot Who Can't Walk Builds a New Life One Step at a Time
video_description: This story follows Gina, a parrot whose feet were badly broken by a human and healed the wrong way. Unable to perch or walk ...
text_content:
It really angers me knowing Gina's toes and foot bones were broken, and then _she was left to suffer that way._ 

We always hope that only decent, caring humans keep other species as companions, but then we are confronted with the ugly truth. 

Divine intervention occurred when Michele found out about Gina and did everything in her power to bring Gina home with her. 

And Gina knew this, too. Birds can perceive differences between humans, and Gina realized that trustworthy and loving humans _do_ exist. 

It's why she is recovering so well, considering the extent of  permanent disability and disfigurement she was left with. Which was on purpose, it appears.

Having adopted a smaller parrot who went through the same kind of physical abuse and who receiving no treatment and care, I also sought veterinary help for him after freeing him from his captors (Little Star). 

A twisted, hobbling little bird who was the size of a 3-month old instead of an adult, the veterinarian who examined him was holding back tears on realizing someone did to Little Star what had been done to Gina. There was no way to repair the damage once the bones had knit. 

He devised a rehabilitation plan for me to work with Little Star, who also "walked on his legs" instead of his feet. 

These precious birds don't deserve to suffer, let alone purposefully. 

In the end, for however long Gina's life will be, at least she will be safe, loved, given the medical and flock care she needs and deserves, all thanks to a _good_ lady with a humble soul, a big heart, and common sense.

_Thank goodness, thank goodness, thank goodness._

🦜 💔 😢  ----> 👩 ❤ 🦜 😊

video_title: Meet me and the animals! #savannahmonitor #monitorlizard #biglizard #training #socialization #cute
text_content:
Hey there ! Your video was the 1st to pop up & I saw Savannah monitor so I had to watch ! I have to share this with you….your story is EXACTLY like mine ! No joke ! 
My 1st monitor…
My 1st ever reptile was a Savannah monitor named Joanna ! I called her Joanna Savannah 🥰 
& just like Jabba….Joannah was my world ! I got her as a baby….she literally fit in the palm of my hand ! I had her for a little over four years & she got to be almost 2 1/2 feet. But very sadly & very unexpectedly she passed away December of 2023. She literally took her last breath on me as I was holding her up on my shoulder outside in the sunshine where she loved to be ! I had a special plaque made with her footprints on it, and I have another one made with her picture on it. I started my YouTube channel a few years ago because of her ! It still breaks my heart that she’s not around so I can totally relate to you big time ! 
Today I do have another Savannah named JoJo. She’s a character ! I absolutely love Savannahs and will always have them in my life ! 
If you ever produce CBB Savannahs let me know ! 
It’s funny… I also have an Ackie monitor & I used to have two white throats in the past. 
I also have an adult male peach throat monitor ! I got him as a baby & he’s about 3 1/2 years old now. He’s my world ! He’s super friendly and very tame and social ! The bond that him and I have is absolutely incredible !
I noticed your grow tent in the background…. I have my peach throat in one and I think they are amazing for reptile enclosures ! 
I also have 3 leopard geckos & 1 gargoyle gecko as well. 
Didn’t mean to write a book here lol. But wanted to share all of that with you because I see that you are just as passionate about your reptiles as I am !
